sutta: But suppose monks a pool of water lucid tranquil and unstead then the man who has eyes to see while standing on the bank could see the oysters and the shells the pebbles and the gravel as they lie and the shoals of fish that dart about why so because of the untroubled nature of the water monks just so it is possible for that monk of whom I speak with his untroubled mind to understand either his own profit or that of others both his own profit and that of others it is possible for him to realize states surpassing those of ordinary men the excellence of truly ariyan knowledge and insight what is the cause of that the untroubled nature of his mind.
commentary: okay now I have to comment on this this troubled state of the mind and the untroubled state of the mind refers to the five hindrances just now we heard that the uncultivated mind is the troubled mind in some other sutta the Buddha gave a very clear simile for the five hindrances suppose a man tries to look for his reflection in water a person with strong sensual desire is like water with dye the water is no longer clear so he cannot see his reflection for ill will the simile is boiling water the water is so disturbed you cannot see your reflection for sloth and torpor it is like water covered with weeds you cannot see through it for restlessness and worry it is like water stirred by wind ripples and waves prevent seeing clearly for doubt it is like muddy water placed in a dark place you cannot see anything at all so here the Buddha is saying the turbid state of water is like the troubled mind a mind full of hindrances cannot see clearly clear still water is like a cultivated mind a tranquil mind with strong samādhi clear bright and still.
